segments.caption: The video opens with the PBS logo on a dark blue background, transitioning to the PBS NOVA logo with a stylized brain graphic, setting a scientific and exploratory tone. The scene shifts to a 3D model of a human brain with glowing neural activity, overlaid on a grid of MRI scans, suggesting a focus on neuroscience. The narrative then moves to diverse human experiences: a young man walking outdoors with headphones, a woman stretching on a bridge, a saxophonist playing in a dimly lit room, and a man browsing books in a library. A close-up of feet walking on a railroad track at sunset evokes a sense of journey or introspection. The video continues with a man working at a desk with a laptop, a woman reading a book with a yellow sticky note, and a hand flipping through pages of a dense academic text discussing consciousness. The scene transitions to a person rock climbing in a rugged canyon, followed by a silhouette of a dancer performing a graceful pose against a vibrant sunset. The final frames show a man speaking directly to the camera in a studio setting, and a woman in a contemplative pose, touching her face, with a blurred background suggesting an indoor environment. The sequence blends scientific inquiry with personal and physical experiences, emphasizing the human mind and body in various contexts.

segments.caption: The video opens with a dynamic, abstract visualization of a 3D brain model, glowing with blue neural pathways and red cellular structures, suggesting intense brain activity. This transitions into a close-up of a woman with curly hair, overlaid with a translucent brain graphic, symbolizing thought or consciousness. The scene shifts to a dimly lit hallway, where a hand reaches out to open a door, creating a sense of mystery or transition. Next, a man in a suit gestures while speaking in a library setting, followed by a split-screen view of two hands drawing identical shapes on paper, hinting at collaboration or mirroring. A historical black-and-white photograph of a man with a tourniquet-like device around his neck appears, overlaid with a brain diagram, suggesting a historical medical or scientific context. The video then cuts to a woman in a red blazer speaking with a blurred, fiery background, followed by a distorted, glitchy image of a brain, emphasizing disruption or digital interference. A woman with curly hair is shown again, this time with a green, networked brain graphic beside her, symbolizing connectivity or AI. The sequence culminates in a cosmic animation with the NOVA logo emerging from a nebula, followed by funding credits on a blue background. The final scenes show a close-up of a metal bowl with a cork, popcorn being poured into it, and a person in a red shirt walking toward a TV displaying a brain, blending domestic life with scientific themes.

segments.caption: The video opens with a woman in a pink blazer holding a blue bowl of popcorn, seated on a checkered couch in a cozy, brick-walled living room, suggesting a relaxed, domestic setting. She smiles warmly as she watches a screen displaying a colorful, abstract animation of a DNA helix, which transitions into a vibrant 3D model of a human brain with glowing neural pathways. The scene shifts to a close-up of a neuron firing, followed by a detailed MRI scan of a brain superimposed over a human eye, emphasizing the connection between perception and neural activity. The woman then walks through a modern office hallway, entering a lab where she collaborates with a man on a wooden box experiment, drawing shapes on paper in a split-screen view that mirrors their synchronized actions. The narrative continues with a split-screen showing a person sleeping under a blanket alongside a complex polygraph readout, indicating sleep monitoring. The woman is later seen in a dimly lit lab, observing a man with a red light device, possibly for brain stimulation. The video returns to the living room, where she speaks directly to the camera, her expression thoughtful. The sequence ends with extreme close-ups of a human eye and mouth, followed by intricate mechanical gears, symbolizing the inner workings of the mind and body. Throughout, the PBS NOVA logo remains in the top left corner, anchoring the scientific and educational tone of the segment.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of intricate mechanical gears, symbolizing inner workings, transitioning to a woman in a pink blazer (Woman in Pink Blazer) sitting on a checkered couch in a cozy, brick-walled living room, holding a blue bowl of popcorn. She gestures expressively as she speaks, suggesting a narrative or explanation. The scene shifts to a serene sunset over mountains with the title 'YOUR BRAIN WHO'S IN CONTROL?' overlaid, followed by a night sky with a full moon. A man with curly hair is shown in a bathroom, leaning over a sink, hands on his face, reflecting distress. The Woman in Pink Blazer reappears, now in a black blazer, speaking directly to the camera against a brick background. The video cuts to a classic alarm clock on a bedside table, then to a woman in bed, tossing and turning, with text identifying the editor, Ryan Shepheard. The scene transitions to a man in bed, eyes wide open in a dimly lit room, with text identifying the host, Heather Berlin. A night-vision shot shows a man suddenly sitting up in bed, screaming, with on-screen text capturing his vocalizations. The final scene features a man in a suit (Emmanuel During, MD) speaking in an office setting, with his name and affiliation displayed on screen.

The final scene shows a woman in a black coat walking on a rainy city sidewalk with an umbrella, entering a building labeled 'Mount Sinai Union Square Care Center' through glass doors, with reflections of the wet street visible on the glass.

segments.caption: The video opens with a woman in a black coat walking down a modern, well-lit office hallway with glass walls, entering a room labeled 'Mount Sinai Union Square Care Center.' She meets with another woman in a black top, seated at a wooden desk adorned with a 3D brain model, a tablet, and office supplies, engaging in a serious conversation. The camera alternates between close-ups of their expressive faces and wider shots of the office, which features plants, computers, and a corkboard with notes. The scene transitions to the woman in the black coat walking down a hospital corridor with a man in a suit, then entering a sleep lab where a patient is being fitted with EEG electrodes. The patient lies in bed, connected to monitoring equipment, while a technician adjusts the setup. The video then cuts to a control room where two individuals analyze sleep data on large screens displaying complex waveforms, including EEG, ECG, and muscle activity traces. A split-screen view shows the real-time data alongside a night-vision camera feed of the sleeping patient, highlighting physiological changes during sleep stages. The segment concludes with a close-up of the sleep monitoring screen, emphasizing the detailed polygraph readout, with labels such as 'NREM 3' and 'Chin 2-Chin 1,' indicating deep sleep and muscle tone monitoring.

segments.caption: The segment begins with a close-up of a polygraph readout screen displaying multiple colored waveforms, including EEG, ECG, and muscle activity, labeled with technical annotations such as 'NREM 3' and 'Chin 2-Chin 1', indicating a sleep study. The scene transitions to a dark, blue-toned night-vision shot of a person sleeping, overlaid with a yellow waveform and a dashed line marking the onset of 'DEEP SLEEP'. The video then cuts to a control room where a man in a dark suit and light blue tie (Man in Suit) and a woman with long dark hair (Woman in Black Coat) are seated, observing the sleep data on a large monitor. The screen shows a split view: the left side displays the polygraph readout with fluctuating waveforms and numerical data, while the right side shows a night-vision camera feed of a person in bed. The person in bed suddenly sits up, appearing agitated, and then begins to physically interact with another person lying beside them, who is also wearing EEG electrodes. The man in the suit speaks to the woman, likely discussing the observed event. The segment concludes with a stylized 3D brain model, highlighting the 'PREFRONTAL CORTEX' in red, suggesting a focus on brain regions involved in sleep and consciousness. The PBS NOVA logo remains consistently in the top left corner throughout.

segments.caption: The segment begins with a stylized 3D brain model against a dark background, highlighting the 'PREFRONTAL CORTEX' and 'POSTERIOR PARIETAL CORTEX' in red, with the PBS NOVA logo in the top left. The scene transitions to a man in a dark suit and light blue tie holding a realistic brain model, gesturing as he speaks in what appears to be a lab or office setting. The video then cuts to a kitchen scene where a woman in a pink floral dress walks toward the stove, turns, and suddenly stumbles, losing her balance—her movement is erratic and uncoordinated. This is followed by another brain diagram, this time with the 'MOTOR CORTEX' and 'VISUAL CORTEX' highlighted in yellow, then expanding to include 'LANGUAGE' and 'BALANCE' regions, visually linking the woman’s physical instability to brain function. The woman is shown again in the kitchen, visibly disoriented, turning sharply and appearing confused. The final shot introduces Susana Martinez-Conde, PhD, from SUNY Downstate Health Sciences University, speaking directly to the camera in a well-lit office with large windows, her gray hair styled in soft waves and wearing a floral sleeveless top, as she explains the neuroscience behind the observed behavior.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a woman in a bright yellow hoodie, standing outdoors at dusk, her face expressive as she speaks, with a blurred cityscape and water in the background. The scene transitions to a close-up of hands gently touching a child’s arm, suggesting care or examination. Next, a woman in a blue shirt, identified as Nancy Kanwisher, PhD from MIT McGovern Institute, speaks while gesturing, with a stylized 3D brain model appearing beside her, glowing with neural activity. The brain model then floats in darkness, pulsing with light, symbolizing neural networks. The scene shifts to a woman with long blonde hair, Daniela Schiller, PhD from Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, speaking directly to the camera in a softly lit indoor setting. This is followed by a black-and-white night-vision shot of a man in bed, suddenly jolting awake and screaming, with on-screen text 'no no no no no no...' and 'oh oh oh oh oh oh', indicating a nightmare or sleep disturbance. The video then cuts to a close-up of feet in brown shoes walking on a tiled floor, followed by Heather Berlin, PhD, MPH, also from Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, speaking in front of a brick wall. A brief, blurred shot of a person in a lab setting transitions to Narayanan 'Bobby' Kasthuri, MD, PhD, from the University of Chicago and Argonne National Laboratory, speaking with expressive hand gestures. The scene changes to a woman sleeping peacefully in bed under a blue-toned light, her face serene. Finally, Anil Seth, PhD from the University of Sussex, appears, speaking directly to the camera in a studio setting, gesturing with his hands as he explains a concept. Throughout, the PBS NOVA logo remains in the top left corner, and on-screen text identifies each speaker and their affiliation.

segments.caption: The video opens with a woman in a white lab coat and glasses walking down a dimly lit hospital corridor, her expression serious and focused. The scene transitions to a patient lying on an operating table, receiving a nasal spray from a medical professional in blue gloves, suggesting pre-surgical preparation. The camera then shifts to a close-up of a surgeon in full surgical attire, including a mask and cap, illuminated by a bright surgical light, emphasizing the sterile and intense environment. The woman from the beginning is now seen putting on a surgical mask and cap, preparing for the procedure. She walks down a brightly lit hospital hallway marked 'Lunder 3 Operating Rooms 61-73', joining a team of medical staff in blue scrubs. The team enters an operating room where a patient is being prepped, with electrodes being placed on the patient’s head and an IV bag labeled 'Phenylephrine' being hung. A medical monitor displays vital signs, including a heart rate of 93 bpm. The team administers anesthesia, with a Baxter infusion pump showing 'Propofol' being delivered at a rate of 120 mL/hr. The patient is then intubated, and the medical team continues to monitor and adjust equipment, ensuring the patient is stable under anesthesia. The scene captures the precision, coordination, and high-stakes environment of a surgical procedure, with a focus on the technical and human elements of medical care.

segments.caption: The video opens in a sterile operating room where a medical team in blue scrubs, surgical caps, and masks is preparing a patient for surgery. The patient, lying on the operating table, is initially seen receiving a nasal spray, followed by the placement of an oxygen mask and manual ventilation using a blue bag-valve mask. The team, including a male surgeon with glasses and a gray beard and a female medical professional with a surgical cap, closely monitors the patient’s response. The female professional observes the patient’s eyes, noting they are 'fixed,' indicating a loss of consciousness. The scene shifts to a close-up of a Masimo monitor displaying vital signs, including a brain signal reading of 85, which then drops to 42, suggesting a decline in brain activity. The team proceeds to attach EEG electrodes to the patient’s scalp, labeled with positions like 'F2' and 'C2,' to monitor brain waves. The EEG monitor shows a flatline, and the video transitions to a graphic illustrating different brain wave patterns—'ACTIVE,' 'RELAXED,' and 'REM SLEEP'—to explain the patient’s state. The environment is brightly lit with surgical lights, and the atmosphere is tense and focused, emphasizing the critical nature of the procedure. On-screen text from the PBS NOVA logo remains consistently in the top left corner throughout the segment.

segments.caption: The segment begins with a blackboard-style graphic illustrating brain wave patterns labeled 'ACTIVE,' 'RELAXED,' 'REM SLEEP,' and 'DEEP SLEEP,' each represented by distinct yellow waveforms. The video then zooms in on a single waveform, highlighting 'FREQUENCY' with a horizontal arrow and 'AMPLITUDE' with a vertical arrow, visually explaining the properties of brain waves. The scene transitions to a real-world medical setting, showing two Masimo monitors in an operating room, displaying vital signs including heart rate, blood pressure, and brain wave activity, with one monitor showing a heart rate of 89 and the other 29. The camera then cuts to a close-up of Emery N. Brown, MD, PhD, a bald man with a gray beard and glasses, speaking in an interview setting. The video overlays a brain wave graphic across a split screen, labeling the left side 'CONSCIOUS' and the right 'UNCONSCIOUS,' visually demarcating the transition between states. This is followed by a scene of a group of friends laughing and interacting in a bright, cozy living room, with a yellow brain wave graphic superimposed, symbolizing active consciousness. The scene then shifts to a blurred operating room, where medical staff in blue scrubs and masks are working, with the same brain wave graphic now showing a decline, symbolizing unconsciousness. The video returns to the friends, now blurred, with the brain wave continuing to dip, reinforcing the contrast between conscious and unconscious states. The final frames show the friends' scene fading into a blurred, dreamlike state, with the brain wave pattern becoming irregular and subdued, visually representing the loss of consciousness.

segments.caption: The segment begins with Michael Miller, PhD, holding a detailed anatomical model of a human brain, pointing to its internal structures while explaining its components. The scene transitions to a stylized 3D animation of a brain, which splits into two hemispheres connected by a highlighted yellow structure labeled 'CORPUS CALLOSUM,' visually demonstrating the brain's division and connection. The animation then shows the corpus callosum being severed, symbolizing a split-brain condition. This is followed by archival footage from September 25, 1983, showing a person wearing a specialized eye-tracking device, followed by close-ups of hands performing a task, suggesting a split-brain experiment. The video then cuts to a modern lab setting where Michael Miller, along with two women—one in a beige cardigan and another in a black top—interact around a wooden experimental apparatus with a notebook and pen. Miller gestures toward the setup, explaining the procedure, while the women observe attentively. The environment is a well-lit, professional lab with a monitor displaying a 'Welcome' screen, indicating a controlled experimental setting. The segment blends historical footage with contemporary scientific demonstration, emphasizing the study of brain function and hemispheric specialization.

segments.caption: The video segment opens in a modern laboratory setting where Michael Miller, PhD, a man with a gray beard and curly hair wearing a light blue button-down shirt, is conducting a cognitive experiment with two women. One woman, wearing a beige cardigan and glasses, is seated at a wooden experimental apparatus with a split-screen setup, while the other, in a black top, observes from the side. The apparatus features a wooden box with two openings, each with a notebook and pen, designed to isolate visual input to each hemisphere of the brain. A monitor displays simple geometric shapes—a square and a circle—on a light blue background, which the participant is instructed to draw. As the experiment progresses, the woman in the beige cardigan draws with her right hand (controlled by the left brain) and her left hand (controlled by the right brain), producing two distinct, often asymmetrical shapes. The camera alternates between close-ups of the hands drawing and wider shots of the participants interacting. On-screen text labels the left and right sides of the split-screen as 'LEFT BRAIN' and 'RIGHT BRAIN' to emphasize the hemispheric division. Michael Miller gestures and explains the process, while the women react with smiles and curiosity. The environment is well-lit, with a laptop, water bottle, and cables visible in the background, reinforcing the scientific context. The segment highlights the split-brain phenomenon, demonstrating how each hemisphere processes visual information independently, with the right brain often producing more abstract or creative interpretations, as seen in the differing drawings.

The camera zooms in on the notebooks, revealing two distinct, asymmetrical shapes being drawn simultaneously—one resembling a bell, the other a trapezoid—highlighting independent hemispheric activity. The segment concludes with Michael Gazzaniga, PhD, seated at a desk with a brain model, speaking in an academic setting with bookshelves in the background, providing expert commentary on the split-brain experiments.

segments.caption: The segment begins with Michael Gazzaniga, PhD, seated at a wooden desk in a well-lit academic setting with bookshelves in the background, gesturing with both hands raised as if to emphasize a point, with a 3D brain model on the desk before him. The scene cuts to archival footage of a woman in a light blue shirt instructing a participant, saying, 'If you look right at my nose, I’m going to hold up my hands. You tell me how many fingers you see, alright?' The participant, a woman in a white lab coat, responds to the test. The video then returns to Gazzaniga, now holding up one finger on each hand, illustrating a point. This is followed by an animated diagram showing the left and right visual fields, with corresponding brain hemispheres highlighted in red and green, and the optic nerves crossing. The animation then highlights the corpus callosum in yellow, explaining its role in connecting the hemispheres. The scene cuts back to Gazzaniga, who continues his explanation with the same hand gesture. The archival footage resumes, showing a close-up of hands performing a task, with the text 'You see anything else?' and the participant responding 'No.' The segment concludes with Gazzaniga again, followed by the animated diagram reappearing, now showing a speech bubble icon near the left hemisphere, indicating language processing, and a dashed line through the corpus callosum, symbolizing its severance in split-brain patients. The PBS NOVA logo remains consistently in the top left corner throughout.

segments.caption: The segment begins with a close-up of a computer monitor displaying a sagittal MRI scan of a human brain, viewed from behind a person's head, suggesting a medical or research setting. The scene transitions to a man in a dark suit and light blue shirt, seated in front of a wooden bookshelf, speaking directly to the camera—this is the Man in Suit, likely a neuroscientist or expert. The video then cuts to a stylized black-and-white animation of a human head in profile, with the brain revealed and the insula region highlighted in yellow, labeled 'INSULA,' emphasizing its role in internal bodily awareness. This is followed by a wide shot of a snow-covered college campus, featuring a prominent brick building with a white steeple, establishing an academic environment. The narrative shifts to a close-up of a hand adjusting a mechanical thermostat dial, moving from 70 to 80 degrees, symbolizing temperature regulation. This is mirrored by an animated thermometer graphic, which then connects via a line to the insula in the brain diagram, illustrating the brain’s role in monitoring internal states. The Man in Suit reappears, continuing his explanation. The scene cuts to a digital thermostat being adjusted from 80 to 70 degrees, reinforcing the theme of homeostasis. The animation returns, now showing a connection from the insula to the 'VENTRAL MEDIAL PREFRONTAL CORTEX,' indicating neural pathways involved in decision-making or regulation. The segment concludes with another close-up of the mechanical thermostat being turned, visually linking physical temperature control to the brain’s internal regulation system.

segments.caption: The segment begins with a close-up of a hand adjusting a beige mechanical thermostat, turning the dial from 70 to 60 degrees, symbolizing control over internal states. This transitions to a stylized black-and-white brain diagram highlighting the 'VENTRAL MEDIAL PREFRONTAL CORTEX' in red and the 'INSULA' in yellow, with a glowing connection between them, illustrating neural pathways involved in emotion and decision-making. The scene shifts to a sepia-toned historical photograph of a man in formal 19th-century attire, holding a long iron rod, his face showing a distinct injury above the eye, suggesting a traumatic brain injury. This is followed by a silhouette animation of a man on a railroad track, being struck by a tamping iron, which then pierces his skull, visually narrating the famous case of Phineas Gage. The animation continues with the iron rod exiting the top of the skull, and then cuts to a woman in a dark turtleneck speaking directly to the camera, likely explaining the case. The video then shows a detailed anatomical illustration of a skull with the iron rod passing through, labeled '2', and a cross-section of the brain with the rod’s path marked. The historical photograph reappears, now framed in ornate gold, emphasizing its significance. The woman reappears, continuing her narration, before the scene transitions to a sepia photograph of a railroad construction site with workers, horses, and wooden structures, contextualizing the accident. The segment concludes with the historical photograph of the injured man again, followed by the woman speaking, reinforcing the narrative of Phineas Gage’s story and its implications for neuroscience.

segments.caption: The video opens with Narayanan 'Bobby' Kasthuri, MD, PhD, speaking expressively in a dimly lit room with exposed brick walls, wearing a dark floral shirt and glasses. The scene transitions to a woman with long dark hair, wearing a black blazer, engaged in conversation in a lab setting with computer monitors in the background. Next, a split-screen shows a sleep monitoring screen with polygraph readouts on the left and a night-vision view of a person sleeping in bed on the right, with the person suddenly sitting up and screaming. The scene shifts to an operating room where a surgeon in blue scrubs and a surgical cap, along with a woman in a surgical cap and mask, are focused on a patient. A close-up of a Masimo medical monitor displays vital signs, including a heart rate of 85 bpm and brain wave activity. The video then cuts to a rocky cliff face with a person in a red shirt and helmet climbing, followed by a woman in a striped shirt stretching on a bridge railing. In a gym, a woman in a gray tank top lifts a kettlebell while others watch. The scene changes to a woman with long dark hair, wearing a black top, sitting at a desk with a computer, then to a close-up of hands playing a guitar. A woman in a red blazer and mustard-yellow top, identified as Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, speaks expressively in a modern indoor setting. The video transitions to a woman with short blonde hair in a dark turtleneck, speaking in a softly lit room. A 3D brain model with colorful, heat-map-like regions rotates against a dark background, followed by a woman with curly hair and glasses, speaking outdoors with a translucent brain model superimposed beside her, glowing with a network of connections. The PBS NOVA logo remains consistently in the top left corner throughout.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a black screen displaying the PBS NOVA logo in the top left corner, followed by the title 'YOUR BRAIN WHO'S IN CONTROL?' in bold white text. The scene transitions to a deep blue background with funding credits, listing major supporters such as the Corporation for Public Broadcasting, Alfred P. Sloan Foundation, and George D. Smith Fund, Inc., along with additional support from the NOVA Science Trust and Margaret and Will Hearst. The background then shifts to a rotating 3D brain model with glowing purple and orange neural pathways, serving as a backdrop for the production credits, which include camera operators, producers, editors, and other crew members. As the credits continue, the brain model transitions to a grayscale, particle-based representation with a network of connecting lines, maintaining the same credit format. The background then changes to a dark red, abstract visualization of neural networks, with the credits continuing to roll. Throughout, the PBS NOVA logo remains in the top left, and a DVD advertisement for 'YOUR BRAIN' appears on the right side of the screen, displaying the price ($24.99), purchase options (ShopPBS.org, 1-800-PLAY-PBS), and streaming availability (PBS Passport, Prime Video). The final frames show the credits concluding with legal, business, and senior production roles, all overlaid on the red neural network background, before fading to black.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a series of production credits displayed over a rotating grayscale 3D brain model, which serves as a dynamic backdrop. The text includes roles such as Senior Development Producer, Senior Director of Brand Marketing, and Executive Producers, with names like David Condon, Nadia Petschek Rawls, Pamela Rosenstein, Julia Cort, and Chris Schmidt. The credits continue with copyright and funding information, noting the program was produced by WGBH and funded by the Alfred P. Sloan Foundation and the George D. Smith Fund, Inc., among others. The brain model rotates slowly, its surface textured with subtle light reflections, creating a scientific and cerebral atmosphere. As the credits conclude, the screen fades to black, followed by a brief appearance of the GBH logo in purple and black. The final frames transition to a static promotional screen for NOVA and PBS, featuring the NOVA and PBS logos in blue on a white background, with a diagonal blue gradient on the right side. The screen includes calls to action: 'WATCH ON THE PBS VIDEO APP' and 'FOLLOW US' with social media icons for Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram, along with the handle '@novapbs'. The text 'VIEW MORE' is displayed in the top right corner, suggesting further content is available. The overall tone is professional and informational, designed to conclude the program and direct viewers to additional resources.
